content: Lipid disorders encompass abnormalities in cholesterol and triglyceride metabolism that increase atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease (ASCVD) risk. Major types: (1) Elevated LDL cholesterol - primary driver of atherosclerosis; (2) Low HDL cholesterol - associated with increased CVD risk though raising HDL hasn't shown benefit; (3) Hypertriglyceridemia - independent risk factor, severe levels (>500 mg/dL) cause pancreatitis; (4) Elevated lipoprotein(a) - genetically determined, independent CVD risk factor. Familial hypercholesterolemia (FH): autosomal dominant disorder (LDLR, APOB, PCSK9 mutations) causing very high LDL from birth (heterozygous LDL 190-450 mg/dL, homozygous >450 mg/dL), premature ASCVD, tendon xanthomas, corneal arcus. Prevalence 1:200-250 for heterozygous FH. Secondary causes: hypothyroidism, nephrotic syndrome, cholestatic liver disease, medications (thiazides, beta-blockers, retinoids, immunosuppressants, antiretrovirals).
